text: My name is Mark Glover from Austin, Texas. This is a video of a robotic unoscolar hernia that was present for approximately seven to eight years prior to surgery.

The surgeon laparoscopically reduces the chronically incarcerated ascending and transverse colon, along with a large amount of omentum, from the hernia sac. This reduction is performed prior to docking the robotic system, allowing simultaneous laparoscopic and manual manipulation of the scrotum.
The surgeon uses the shaft of the instrument to apply lateral pressure on the bowel, avoiding direct grasping, while receiving haptic feedback on the pressure and tension applied.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the chronically incarcerated bowel, which took approximately forty-five minutes, before docking the robot to initiate preperitoneal dissection.
Whether laparoscopic or robotic, I typically like to reduce the hernia sac in its entirety. However, in this individual, that is not feasible.
The surgeon isolates the peritoneal sac from the cord structures, then transects the peritoneal sac after completing distal dissection.
These large immunoscoridal hernias often develop a seroma and the patient is informed of this prior to surgery.

The surgeon displays the peritoneal defect where the hernia sac was previously transected. A large heavyweight polypropylene mesh is positioned over the defect and secured in multiple locations using absorbable sutures.
The surgeon fixates the mesh in place and begins closing the peritoneum. After completing the peritoneal closure, the defect is closed, ensuring the mesh is fully excluded from the peritoneal cavity.
The patient was discharged home the same day, returned to work within two weeks.
